skill.name: search-enhancer
skill.description: Enhanced code search guidance for finding symbols, patterns, references, and code quality signals across repositories.
skill.summary: Helps users turn natural language code questions into targeted grep and ripgrep searches.
skill.author: CuriousLearner

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer flagged many command, network, environment, filesystem, and weak-crypto patterns, but review shows they are documentation examples in SKILL.md rather than hidden executable code. The main confirmed risk is that the skill recommends shell commands, including xargs pipelines and replacement examples that can modify files if run without review.

medium_findings.title: Shell Command Guidance Can Modify Workspace Files
medium_findings.description: The skill is mostly documentation, but it recommends shell search workflows and includes replacement pipelines using xargs and sed. These examples are legitimate for code search, but they can change repository files if an agent runs them without user review.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are explicit shell examples for search and replace. The risk is confirmed, although it is a documented developer workflow rather than malicious hidden behavior.

medium_findings.title: Command Substitution Expands Search Scope Dynamically
medium_findings.description: The performance section uses command substitution to pass tracked files into ripgrep. This is a normal developer pattern, but generated shell commands should be reviewed because dynamic expansion can affect many paths.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command substitution is present and executable in a shell. It appears intended for performance, so the concern is operational safety rather than malicious intent.

low_findings.title: Sensitive Term Search Examples Are Not Credential Access
low_findings.description: Several lines search for API keys, tokens, passwords, or environment variable references. These are examples for locating risky code and do not read environment variables or transmit secrets.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The evidence shows literal search strings inside command examples. No code path accesses process environment values or credential stores.

low_findings.title: Network Pattern Is A Search Query Only
low_findings.description: The network finding points to a ripgrep expression for locating fetch or axios calls in source code. It does not import an HTTP library or make a network request.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line is a text search command for API call discovery. There is no outbound endpoint, request payload, or executable network client in the skill.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are Text-Matching False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak-cryptography alerts do not correspond to crypto implementation or hashing guidance in the reviewed context. No evidence found of MD5, SHA-1, or similar weak cryptographic use.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are description text or search documentation, not cryptographic code. A targeted check found no weak crypto terms in SKILL.md.

low_findings.title: Hidden File Reference Is Configuration Documentation
low_findings.description: The hidden-file alerts relate to examples mentioning .gitignore, .ripgreprc, and VS Code search exclusions. These are normal search configuration references, not hidden file harvesting.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The references are configuration examples that help scope search behavior. They do not instruct collection of hidden files or credential material.

dangerous_patterns.title: xargs With sed Replacement Pipeline
dangerous_patterns.description: The documented replacement workflow chains ripgrep results into xargs and sed. This is powerful but can apply broad file edits if used on an imprecise pattern.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The exact pipeline is present in a shell example. The skill labels one variant with caution, which reduces but does not remove the operational risk.

dangerous_patterns.title: Search Result Redirection Writes Files
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes output redirection examples for reports and search exports. These are common workflows, but they write files into the current workspace.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The redirection examples are visible and legitimate. The risk is limited to expected local file writes rather than unauthorized filesystem access.

content.user_title: Find Code Faster With Smarter Search
content.value_statement: Large repositories make important code hard to locate quickly. This skill converts code questions into focused grep, ripgrep, regex, and symbol-search strategies.
actual_capabilities: Translates natural language code questions into targeted search patterns.
Suggests grep and ripgrep commands for files, symbols, imports, and references.
Provides language-specific searches for JavaScript, TypeScript, Python, and Go.
Guides regex searches for functions, classes, hooks, API routes, and TODO markers.
Explains ways to scope searches by directory, file type, and ignored paths.
Includes examples for summarizing, exporting, and grouping search results.
limitations: It is documentation-only and does not include a dedicated search engine.
Some examples require local tools such as grep, ripgrep, find, awk, sed, or xargs.
Regex searches can miss semantic relationships that require language-aware tooling.
Replacement examples can modify files and should be reviewed before execution.

use_cases.title: Locate Feature Code
use_cases.description: Find components, hooks, routes, or helper functions across a repository before changing a feature.

use_cases.title: Audit Risky Patterns
use_cases.description: Search for hardcoded secrets, eval usage, SQL concatenation, or blocking file operations during review.

use_cases.title: Map Repository Structure
use_cases.description: Identify imports, models, interfaces, and related symbols when learning an unfamiliar codebase.

prompt_templates.title: Find A Basic Term
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner search for a known keyword or symbol.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use search-enhancer to find every occurrence of [term] in [directory]. Show the most relevant files first.

prompt_templates.title: Search By Language Pattern
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate search for a known code shape.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use search-enhancer to find [functions/classes/components/hooks] in [language] files under [path]. Include a focused regex.

prompt_templates.title: Trace References
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced search for usages and related imports.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use search-enhancer to locate the definition, imports, and call sites for [symbol]. Group results by relationship.

prompt_templates.title: Build A Review Query Set
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced audit workflow for multiple risk patterns.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use search-enhancer to design searches for [risk area]. Include safe scopes, file type filters, and commands to preview results only.

output_examples.input: Find React components that use useState in the src directory.
output_examples.output: The skill would suggest a scoped ripgrep search for React hook usage in JSX and TSX files, then group matches by component file.

output_examples.input: Find possible hardcoded secrets before release.
output_examples.output: The skill would provide searches for password, token, secret, and API key assignments, with guidance to inspect results carefully.

output_examples.input: Trace where the authentication helper is defined and used.
output_examples.output: The skill would search for the symbol definition, imports, direct calls, and related authentication terms across application paths.

best_practices: Start with a narrow directory and expand only when results are sparse.
Preview search and replacement matches before running commands that write files.
Use file type filters to reduce noise and improve result quality.
anti_patterns: Running broad replacement commands without reviewing the matched files first.
Searching the entire repository when a feature directory or file type is known.
Treating regex matches as complete semantic proof without reading nearby context.

faq.question: Does this skill execute searches automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides search strategies and command examples for an AI assistant or user to apply.

faq.question: Which tools does it focus on?
faq.answer: It focuses on grep, ripgrep, find, shell pipelines, and IDE search concepts.

faq.question: Can it help with semantic code search?
faq.answer: Yes. It suggests symbol, reference, import, and pattern searches, but it does not include an AST engine.

faq.question: Is it safe for large repositories?
faq.answer: Yes, when searches are scoped. Use file type filters and ignored paths to keep results manageable.

faq.question: Can it modify files?
faq.answer: The skill includes replacement examples that can modify files. Review matches and prefer dry runs first.

faq.question: Does it access secrets or send network requests?
faq.answer: No evidence found. Secret and network terms appear as search examples, not as data access or exfiltration code.
